title: Hops and valerian sleep tea
subtitle: A sedative blend for difficulty falling asleep
excerpt: A blend of dried hops flowers and valerian root steeped as an infusion before bed. A traditional European sleep preparation. Both herbs are sedatives; the combination is used for difficulty falling asleep with a restless, over-active mind.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve (the early 20th-century botanical writer), A Modern Herbal, hop entry and valerian entry: the sedative and sleep tradition, the European folk combinations. the herbal reference on Lupuli flos, the well-established use for sleep disturbance. the herbal reference on Valerianae radix (2006), the well-established use for mild sleep disturbance and nervous tension.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Dried hops and valerian root each store 12 months in sealed tins, cool and dark. Prepare each cup fresh; the sedative aromatic oils degrade in stored infusions.

glossaryTerms.definition: A herb that calms nervous activity and promotes drowsiness, used in herbal medicine for mild insomnia and restlessness. Stronger in action than a simple nervine; valerian and hops are the traditional examples.

content.text: The traditional European herbal sleep blend pairs hops with valerian. Both are

with a good evidence base for mild sleep disturbance. Hops (Humulus lupulus) are milder in sedative action than valerian alone. Use this combination when the difficulty is in falling asleep. The herbal tradition records the combination as more effective than either herb alone. The infusion is slightly bitter from the hop resins and earthy from the valerian; accept both tastes as part of the preparation.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Sedatives. Both hops and valerian potentiate benzodiazepines, barbiturates, antihistamines, sleeping tablets, and other CNS depressants. Do not combine with any sedating medication without consulting your prescriber.

Alcohol. Do not combine with alcohol on the same evening; the sedative effect is additive.

Driving. Do not drive on the morning after a night-time dose if you feel residual drowsiness. On first use, take only at bedtime until you know how the blend affects you.

Depression. Hops can deepen low mood in susceptible readers and is traditionally avoided as a sleep remedy in active depression. If you have depression, consult a herbalist before using this blend.

Pregnancy. Hops and valerian are both avoided in pregnancy at therapeutic doses. Consult a herbalist or midwife before any use in pregnancy.

Paediatric use. Not for children under twelve without specialist herbalist advice. Not a safe preparation for infants or young children.

items.prepNote: dried hops flower cones (strobiles); about half a teaspoon; the bitter lupulones are the active constituent
dried and cut valerian root; about half a teaspoon; the earthy smell is correct and expected
optional; the infusion is quite bitter

content.text: Place both dried herbs in a teapot with a lid. Pour over the just-boiled water. Put the lid on immediately and steep covered for 10 minutes. The volatile active compounds of both herbs are volatile; keep the lid on throughout. Strain into a cup. If using honey, add now. Drink warm 30-45 minutes before bed, while winding down.

One cup (containing 1 g hops + 1 g valerian root) 30-45 minutes before bed. The The standard dose range is 0.5-1 g dried hops and 0.3-1 g dried valerian root per cup. Take only at bedtime. Do not take during the day when you need to be alert. Use for up to 4 weeks. If sleep does not improve, speak to your GP. Persistent insomnia warrants investigation of underlying causes. Paediatric use: not for children under twelve.

Prepare each cup fresh. Dried hops and dried valerian root each store 12 months in separate sealed tins, cool and dark. Hops lose potency faster than most herbs; check the smell before use (it should be hoppy and resinous, not musty).
When not to use this remedy

On any sedating medication. After alcohol. In depression. In pregnancy. When you need to drive the next morning. Persistent sleep disturbance affecting daily functioning, sleep with frequent waking associated with pain or breathlessness, or daytime sleepiness despite adequate night sleep all warrant medical investigation.
